positional_bias.note: max_share is exactly 0.40 — not strictly greater than threshold; passes. Sidecar distribution [1,3,0,2,1] confirmed against .gift parse.

length_bias.note: Builder meta reported longest_option_correct_count=2 (40%). Direct re-derivation from .gift text yields 1 question (q5 only) where correct exceeds 120% of distractor mean. Actual rate = 0.20, well under the 0.40 fail threshold. The builder overcounted. Rate is soft-noted only — no fail.

weakest_questions.note: d1 (counting outcomes/tokens) and d2 (listing sample space/multiplying independent probabilities) are plausible-but-weak (score 2 — wrong but not sharply misconception-mapped). Not absurd; they function as recognisable foils for a beginner.

lo_coverage.text: Describe in plain language what Bayes' theorem does — updates an existing belief when new evidence arrives
Explain the meaning of the three named pieces: prior, likelihood, and posterior
Use the worked rain-and-clouds example to trace prior to posterior
Explain why Bayes' theorem is conditional probability reorganised — P(A|B) in terms of P(B|A)
Give one everyday example and one AI-relevant example of Bayesian updating in action

beginner_fairness_and_scope.notes: All stems are self-contained; P(A|B) notation is grounded in the explicitly listed prerequisite (topic 7.2). Every question tests only material taught in this topic's corpus. The LLM/gradient-descent distractor in q5 is drawn from corpus §7 itself and is correctly labelled as out-of-scope for Bayesian updating — not unfair jargon.

verdict: Pass — all five checks clean. Positional max_share exactly 0.40 (at boundary, not over). Length-bias actual rate 0.20 (builder meta overcounted at 2; re-derived count is 1). Distractor absurd_rate 0.00. All five LOs covered 1:1. No scope or fairness issues.
